{{Short description|First successful model of the hydrogen atom incorporating quantum theory}}
{{Infobox physics
| name = Bohr model
| image = Bohr atom model.svg
| caption = The Bohr model: electrons orbit the nucleus in discrete circular orbits with quantized angular momentum.
| topics = [[Atomic physics]], [[Quantum mechanics]], [[Hydrogen atom]], [[Atomic nucleus]]
| proposed_by = [[Niels Bohr]], 1913
}}

The '''Bohr model''' (or '''Rutherford–Bohr model''') is a planetary model of the [[atom]] proposed by [[Niels Bohr]] in 1913. It was the first model to successfully incorporate [[quantum mechanics|quantum theory]] into atomic structure, explaining the spectral lines of [[hydrogen]] and [[hydrogen-like atom]]s.

== Postulates ==
# Electrons orbit the [[atomic nucleus|nucleus]] in stable, circular orbits without radiating energy (contrary to classical electrodynamics)
# Only orbits with quantized [[angular momentum]] are allowed: <math>L = n\hbar</math>, where <math>n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots</math>
# Electrons emit or absorb [[photon]]s when transitioning between orbits: <math>\Delta E = h\nu = E_i - E_f</math>

== Energy levels ==
The allowed energy levels for the hydrogen atom:
:<math>E_n = -\frac{m_e e^4}{8\varepsilon_0^2 h^2}\frac{1}{n^2} = -\frac{13.6\ \text{eV}}{n^2}</math>

The ground state (<math>n = 1</math>) energy is −13.6 eV, the ionization energy of hydrogen.

== Bohr radius ==
The radius of the lowest orbit ([[Bohr radius]]) is:
:<math>a_0 = \frac{4\pi\varepsilon_0 \hbar^2}{m_e e^2} = 5.29 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{m} = 0.529\ \text{Å}</math>

== Spectral series ==
Transitions to specific levels produce named [[spectral series]]:
* [[Lyman series]] — transitions to <math>n = 1</math> (ultraviolet)
* [[Balmer series]] — transitions to <math>n = 2</math> (visible)
* [[Paschen series]] — transitions to <math>n = 3</math> (infrared)

== Limitations ==
* Cannot explain spectra of multi-electron atoms
* Cannot explain [[fine structure]] or [[hyperfine structure]]
* Does not account for [[electron spin]]
* Contradicts the [[uncertainty principle]] (simultaneous position and momentum)
* Replaced by the [[Schrödinger equation|wave mechanical model]] in 1926

Despite its limitations, the Bohr model remains pedagogically valuable for introducing quantum concepts.

== See also ==
{{Portal|Physics}}
* [[Atomic physics]]
* [[Hydrogen atom]]
* [[Quantum mechanics]]
* [[Schrödinger equation]]

== References ==
{{Reflist|30em}}

[[Category:Atomic physics]]
[[Category:Quantum mechanics]]
[[Category:Obsolete theories in physics]]
